content.text: JSX is a syntax extension to JavaScript that lets you write HTML-like markup inside a
) file. It is not HTML and it is not a string — it is syntactic sugar that a compiler (Babel or SWC) transforms into

attrs.alt: JSX compiled to React.createElement with attribute and structure rules

content.text: Understanding this transformation explains every JSX rule — the syntax is designed so that the compiler can map it cleanly to function calls.
A component's return value must be a single expression. You cannot return two adjacent elements:
Wrap siblings in a container element or in a Fragment. Fragments don't add a DOM node:
HTML allows unclosed void elements like
. JSX does not — every tag must be explicitly closed or self-closed:

// ✗ HTML-style — not valid in JSX
// ✔ JSX requires explicit closing
Custom components also self-close when they have no children:
JSX attributes map to JavaScript object properties, not HTML attribute strings. That means HTML's hyphenated and reserved names change:
class is a JS reserved word
for is a JS reserved word
data-* and aria-* keep their hyphens
Rule 4: Expressions Go in Curly Braces
Curly braces open a JavaScript expression context inside JSX. Any valid JavaScript expression — variable, function call, arithmetic, ternary — can go inside them.
(values that evaluate to something) are allowed inside
String literals go in quotes; everything else goes in curly braces:
) cannot appear directly inside JSX markup. Wrap them in a JSX expression:
attribute accepts a JavaScript object, not a CSS string. Property names are camelCase; values are strings or numbers (numbers get
appended automatically for numeric length properties):
You can spread an object of props onto an element using the spread operator. This is useful for forwarding props from a parent:
) can pass unknown HTML attributes to DOM elements and generate browser warnings. Destructure known props out first, and spread only the remainder.
JavaScript's automatic semicolon insertion (ASI) can silently break a multi-line return if the opening tag is on the next line. Wrapping JSX in parentheses prevents this:
inside curly braces to render a list of elements. Each element in the list needs a
prop that uniquely identifies it within its siblings:

prop tells React which element is which across re-renders. Use a stable unique identifier (database ID, slug) — not the array index, which shifts when items are added or removed and causes subtle bugs.
After compilation, JSX becomes a function call — and function calls are expressions. This means JSX can be stored in variables, passed to functions, and returned conditionally:
Before React 17, every file that used JSX had to import React explicitly because the compiler output referenced
. React 17 introduced the new JSX transform, which imports a helper from
Vite and Create React App configure this automatically. You still import
when you use React APIs directly (
, etc.), just not for JSX alone.
Adjacent JSX elements without a wrapper

// ✗ SyntaxError: Expected corresponding JSX closing tag

Hello

// ✔ Every tag closed

Hello

JSX handles whitespace differently from HTML. Leading and trailing whitespace on a line is trimmed; blank lines between elements are removed. To force a space between inline elements, write it explicitly:
